title: Five frontier labs near August 1 deadline on first shared jailbreak-scoring scale
subtitle: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and Amazon are finalizing a common vulnerability-scoring framework — a CVSS-style rubric for AI jailbreaks — under a Trump executive order deadline.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The White House and five frontier AI labs — Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and Amazon — are in the final stretch of talks on a shared scoring system for AI jailbreak vulnerabilities, targeting an August 1 announcement. The framework is being modeled on CVSS, the two-decade-old Common Vulnerability Scoring System that gave the cybersecurity industry a shared vocabulary for software flaws. The deadline is written into President Trump's June 2 executive order on AI innovation and security, which also gave the federal government up to thirty days of pre-release access to designated frontier models. Anthropic publicly called for a shared industry framework after its Fable 5 jailbreak incident in June triggered the Commerce Department's first-ever export control on a model.

The value of a scoring system is not the score. It is the shared vocabulary. Right now, when a security researcher reports a jailbreak to a frontier lab, the lab and the researcher and the customer all use different words to describe severity — sometimes deliberately different words. A common scale forces a lab that ships a broken safeguard to defend a number, not a narrative. That is exactly the pressure CVSS put on software vendors starting in 2005, and it is the reason patch cycles across the industry compressed from months to days.

The politics are unusually clean. All five labs have a commercial interest in the same standard: an enterprise buyer who cannot compare jailbreak severity across models has no rational way to pick between them, so procurement stalls. A shared score lets the buyer compare — and lets a lab that scores well use that as a marketing surface. The dissent, when it comes, will be about who runs the scoring authority: an industry consortium, a government agency, or a hybrid. That question is unresolved, and it will determine whether the framework has teeth or becomes another voluntary badge.

A takeaway for learners: if you are working on any application that touches an LLM, learn to think about safety the way security engineers think about CVEs. Jailbreaks are not a novelty risk anymore — they are a class of vulnerability that will be scored, published, and patched on public timelines. Following the coming CJS-style framework as it lands will teach you how to read AI-model risk the way an infosec engineer reads a CVE feed, which is a skill your future employers will assume you have.

sources.title: AI Model Safety Standards Deal Targets August 1: Five Labs Adopt First Jailbreak Scoring Scale — TechTimes
Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security — The White House
